title: Paper yarn from torn strips
subtitle: Twisting narrow paper strips into a strong, flexible cord for weaving, wrapping, and mixed-media work
excerpt: Paper yarn is made by tearing or cutting paper into narrow strips and twisting them tightly, with moisture to aid flexibility. The result is a surprisingly strong cord, strong enough to weave, knot, and use as a structural element in bookbinding, gift wrapping, and mixed-media collage. Tissue paper produces the finest yarn; newspaper and magazine paper produce a mid-weight cord.
sourceNotes: General craft tradition. Techniques for paper cord production are documented in early 20th-century Japanese craft manuals (mizuhiki and papier-cord traditions) and in Western handcraft literature from the same period.

glossaryTerms.definition: A cord made by twisting narrow paper strips, sometimes with a slight dampness to increase flexibility and bond the twist. Used in weaving, macramé-style knotting, bookbinding ties, and decorative wrapping.

The direction in which the majority of fibres lie in a machine-made sheet, set during the drying process on the moving wire. Paper tears cleanly along the grain and folds sharply across it. For paper yarn, always tear or cut strips parallel to the grain, against-grain strips split unevenly.

A twist direction in which the yarn's surface spirals from lower-left to upper-right, matching the diagonal of the letter Z. The counterpart is S-twist (lower-right to upper-left). Always twist a paper strip in one direction consistently; switching direction untwists the previous work.

content.text: depends almost entirely on the paper used. Tissue at 17-20 gsm twists most easily and produces the finest cord, suitable for stitching and fine weaving. Newspaper at 40-50 gsm produces a mid-weight cord. Cartridge paper at 90 gsm and above is too stiff to twist smoothly without dampening first.

. Tear a small test strip in each direction: the cleaner tear runs parallel to the grain. For standard A4 tissue paper, the grain typically runs parallel to the long edge. Cut or tear strips along the grain, not across it.

Cut strips parallel to the grain at 15-20 mm width for fine yarn, 30-40 mm for mid-weight cord. Longer strips give more working length before a join is needed. Cut the full sheet into strips before beginning to twist, stopping mid-yarn to cut more strips loses momentum and changes the twist tension.
Pinch one end of the strip between the thumb and forefinger of the left hand. With the right hand, roll the strip between thumb and index finger in one direction only, this is the
direction if rolling away from the body.
Work forward along the strip in 5-10 cm sections, rolling and then sliding the left-hand pinch forward to hold the new twist in place.
To join a new strip, overlap the end of the finished strip with the beginning of the new one by 3-4 cm and continue rolling. The twist locks the overlap in place; no adhesive is needed.

items.symptom: The twist keeps unrolling before it can be used
items.cause: The yarn has not been anchored at the end, or the paper is too stiff.
items.fix: Dampen the strip very slightly with clean water before twisting, this makes the fibres more pliable and the twist sets better. Anchor the finished end around a pencil to hold the tension while you work.

items.symptom: The strip tears halfway along during twisting
items.cause: Cutting or tearing across the grain rather than along it.
items.fix: Re-identify the grain direction and re-cut. Against-grain strips have shorter fibre continuity and tear easily under torsion.
